solution:
Given: fx=x−1; x is even2x: x is odd

And fffa=21

⇒fa=a−1=Odd

⇒ffa=2a−1=Even

⇒fffa=2a−3=21

⇒a=12

Case-II: If a is odd

⇒fa=2a=Even

⇒ffa=2a−1=Odd

⇒fffa=4a−2=21 (Not possible)

Hence, a=12

Now,limx→12−x312−x12=limx→12−x312−limx→12−x12

⇒limx→12−x312−x12=144-0

⇒limx→12−x312−x12=144

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let [·] denote the greatest integer function. We have $f(x) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n^3} \sum_{k=1}^n [\frac{k^2}{3^x}]$.
For large $n$: $\sum_{k=1}^n [\frac{k^2}{3^x}] \approx \sum_{k=1}^n \frac{k^2}{3^x} = \frac{1}{3^x} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6} \approx \frac{n^3}{3 \cdot 3^x}$
Therefore: $f(x) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n^3} \cdot \frac{n^3}{3 \cdot 3^x} = \frac{1}{3^{x+1}}$
Computing the infinite series:
$\sum_{j=1}^\infty f(j) = \sum_{j=1}^\infty \frac{1}{3^{j+1}} = \frac{1}{3^2} + \frac{1}{3^3} + \frac{1}{3^4} + \cdots$
$= \frac{1}{9} \cdot (1 + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{9} + \cdots) = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 1/3} = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{2} = \frac{1}{6}$
Therefore: $12 \sum_{j=1}^\infty f(j) = 12 \cdot \frac{1}{6} = 2$

solution:

Let y=n+1n+2….  n+2nn2n1n

y=n+1n·n+2n……n+2nn1n

logy=1nlog1+1n+log1+2n+…..log1+2nn

=1n∑r=12nln1+r/n

Replace ∑→∫ & rn→x & 1n→dx

As n→∞

logy= ∫02log1+xdx

Integrating by parts, we get,

logy=∫021·log1+xdx

=x·log1+x02-∫0211+x xdx

=xlog1+x02- ∫021+x1+xdx+ ∫0211+xdx

=xlog1+x02-x02+log1+x02

=2log3-0-2-0+log3-log1

=3log3-2

Since logy =3log3-2=log27-log e2

=y=elog27e2=27e2

=27e2.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $L = \lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{x^2\sin^2 x}{x^2 - \sin^2 x}$

Dividing the numerator and the denominator by $x^4$, we get:

$L = \lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{\sin x}{x}\right)^2}{\dfrac{x^2 - \sin^2 x}{x^4}}$

Factorizing the denominator:

$L = \lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{\sin x}{x}\right)^2}{\left(\dfrac{x - \sin x}{x^3}\right) \left(\dfrac{x + \sin x}{x}\right)}$

Using the standard limits:

$\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin x}{x} = 1$

$\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{x - \sin x}{x^3} = \dfrac{1}{6}$ (using Taylor series expansion $\sin x = x - \dfrac{x^3}{3!} + \dots$)

$\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{x + \sin x}{x} = \lim_{x \to 0} \left(1 + \dfrac{\sin x}{x}\right) = 1 + 1 = 2$

Substituting these limits into the expression:

$L = \dfrac{1^2}{\left(\dfrac{1}{6}\right) \times 2} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{3}} = 3$

Answer: $3$

subject: Mathematics
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \alpha=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty}\left(\left(\frac{\mathrm{e}}{1-\mathrm{e}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}}\right)\right)^{\mathrm{x}}\left(1^{\infty} \text { form }\right) \\ & \therefore \alpha=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{L}} \\ & \text { Where } \mathrm{L}=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \mathrm{x}\left(\left(\frac{\mathrm{e}}{1-\mathrm{e}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}}\right)-1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{\mathrm{e}}{1-\mathrm{e}}\right) \mathrm{x}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}}-\left(\frac{1-\mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{e}}\right)\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{e}}{1-\mathrm{e}} \lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow \infty} \mathrm{x}\left(1-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{e}}{1-\mathrm{e}} \lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}+1} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~L}=\frac{\mathrm{e}}{1-\mathrm{e}} \cdot 1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~L}=\frac{\mathrm{e}}{1-\mathrm{e}} \\ & \therefore \alpha=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{e}}{1-\mathrm{e}}} \Rightarrow \log \alpha=\frac{\mathrm{e}}{1-\mathrm{e}} \\ & \therefore \text { Required value }=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{e}}{1-\mathrm{e}}}{1+\frac{\mathrm{e}}{1-\mathrm{e}}}=\mathrm{e}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned}
& \lim _{t \rightarrow x} \frac{t^2 f(x)-x^2 f(t)}{t-x}=1 \\
& \lim _{t \rightarrow x} \frac{2 t . f(x)-x^2 f^{\prime}(x)}{1}=1 \\
& 2 x \cdot f(x)-x 2 f^{\prime}(x)=1 \\
& \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{2}{x} \cdot y=\frac{-1}{x^2} \\
& \text { I.f. }=e^{\int-\frac{2}{x} d x}=\frac{1}{x^2} \\
& \therefore \frac{y}{x^2}=\int-\frac{1}{x^4} d x+C \\
& \frac{y}{x^2}=\frac{1}{3 x^3}+C
\end{aligned}$
Put $f(1)=1$
$\begin{aligned} & C=\frac{2}{3} \\ & y=\frac{1}{3 x}+\frac{2 x^2}{3} \\ & y=\frac{2 x^3+1}{3 x} \\ & f(2)=\frac{17}{6} \\ & f(3)=\frac{55}{9} \\ & 2 f(2)+3 f(3)=\frac{17}{3}+\frac{55}{3}=\frac{72}{3}=24\end{aligned}$

=(x-2p)2

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$f(x)$ is a positive increasing function
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 0 < f(x) < f(2 x) < f(3 x) \\
& \Rightarrow 0 < 1 < \frac{f(2 x)}{f(x)} < \frac{f(3 x)}{f(x)} \\
& \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} 1 \leq \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f(2 x)}{f(x)} \leq \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f(3 x)}{f(x)}
\end{aligned}
$$
By sandwich theorem.
$$
\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f(2 x)}{f(x)}=1
$$
